title: [TailCallElim] Add flow-sensitivity to alloca escape analysis
author: FedericoBruzzone
description:
This patch enhances the `AllocaDerivedValueTracker` within the `TailRecursionElimination` (TRE) pass by introducing flow-sensitivity via `DominatorTree`. 

Currently, the pass is overly conservative: any `store` of an alloca-derived pointer into another memory location is treated as an unconditional escape. This prevents TRE even when the address is stored in a local stack slot and never accessed again in a way that would reach the recursive call.

### Logic Changes
The improved analysis distinguishes between safe internal memory usage and actual escapes using the following logic:

* **DominatorTree Integration**: The tracker now utilizes dominance information to verify the scope of alloca usage.
* **Transitive Tracking**: When a tracked pointer is stored into another `alloca`, the analysis now recursively tracks the destination `alloca` instead of marking the source as escaped.
* **Flow-Sensitive Use Filtering**: For any memory location containing a leaked address, we only consider uses that are **dominated** by the `store` instruction. 



> **Rationale**: Any `load` occurring chronologically before the `store` (i.e., not dominated by it) cannot observe the address being tracked. This provides a degree of flow-sensitivity that avoids false-positive escapes from previous, unrelated life-cycles of the memory slot (e.g., stack slot reuse).


### Impact
This change enables Tail Call Elimination for a wider range of patterns:
1. Temporary stack slots used for pointer manipulation.
2. Pointer re-initialization within a function.
3. Transitive pointer storage (e.g., `&A` stored in `B`, then `B` loaded to access `A` locally).

### Testing
Added new test cases to `test/Transforms/TailCallElim/`:
* `alloca-forward-dominance-reuse.ll`: Verifies that immediate store/load cycles do not block TRE. [Compiler Explorer](https://godbolt.org/z/39WTbdT33)
* `alloca-temporal-alias.ll`: Verifies that storing a pointer in an alias slot used only locally is handled correctly. [Compiler Explorer](https://godbolt.org/z/GszMv6Y61)
* `tre-stats-improvement.ll`: Uses the LLVM statistics infrastructure to verify the fix across multiple functions.
* Verified that existing `TailCallElim` regression tests continue to pass.

### Future Work
* **Alias Analysis Integration**: Broaden the scope to track stores into non-alloca destinations that can be proven not to capture via `AA`.
* **Liveness Analysis**: Identify cases where a leaked address is "killed" (overwritten) by a subsequent store before reaching the call site.
* **Aggregate Support**: Extend tracking to handle fields of structures and array elements accessed via `GEP`.

tests.test_body:
define i32 @test_alloca_reuse(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %temp_slot = alloca ptr, align 8
  %dummy = alloca i32, align 4
  store ptr %dummy, ptr %temp_slot, align 8
  %val = load ptr, ptr %temp_slot, align 8
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @test_alloca_reuse(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

define i32 @test_temporary_alias(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %local_var = alloca i32, align 4
  %ptr_storage = alloca ptr, align 8
  store ptr %local_var, ptr %ptr_storage, align 8
  %addr = load ptr, ptr %ptr_storage, align 8
  store i32 42, ptr %addr, align 4
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @test_temporary_alias(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

declare void @opaque(ptr)

define i32 @prevent_tre_on_load_escape(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %local_var = alloca i32, align 4
  %ptr_storage = alloca ptr, align 8
  store ptr %local_var, ptr %ptr_storage, align 8
  %addr = load ptr, ptr %ptr_storage, align 8
  call void @opaque(ptr %addr)
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @prevent_tre_on_load_escape(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

declare void @callback(ptr nocapture readonly)

define i32 @recursive_with_callback(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %data = alloca i32, align 4
  %holder = alloca ptr, align 8
  store i32 %acc, ptr %data, align 4
  store ptr %data, ptr %holder, align 8
  call void @callback(ptr %holder)
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %val = load i32, ptr %data, align 4
  %acc_inc = add i32 %val, 1
  %res = call i32 @recursive_with_callback(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  %result = load i32, ptr %data, align 4
  ret i32 %result
}

define i32 @func3(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %c = alloca i32, align 4
  %ptr = alloca ptr, align 8
  store ptr %c, ptr %ptr, align 8
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @func3(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

define i32 @func1(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %a = alloca i32, align 4
  %ptr = alloca ptr, align 8
  store ptr %a, ptr %ptr, align 8
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @func1(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

define i32 @func2(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %b = alloca i32, align 4
  %ptr = alloca ptr, align 8
  store ptr %b, ptr %ptr, align 8
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %entry
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n, 1
  %acc_inc = add i32 %acc, 1
  %res = call i32 @func2(i32 %n_dec, i32 %acc_inc)
  ret i32 %res

exit:                                             ; preds = %entry
  ret i32 %acc
}

comments.author: FedericoBruzzone
comments.body:
Thank you very much for the insightful catch @antoniofrighetto!

You were absolutely right: the previous implementation had a blind spot regarding the LoadInst. While I was correctly tracking the address into the temporary alloca via the StoreInst, the tracker was prematurely stopping at the load.

I've fixed this by allowing the tracker to propagate through the LoadInst.

I’ve added your example into a new test file: `prevent-tre-alloca-escape.ll`.

comments.author: FedericoBruzzone
comments.body:
> I don't understand the purpose of this PR. You need to justify why this is necessary for canonical IR that has already gone through SROA.

Thank you for your prompt feedback @nikic.

This is my first PR to LLVM, and I hope it won't be my last.
I would like to point out that I am not an expert and am still learning, but I will try to explain what you are asking me.

If I understand correctly, even with the `-O3` optimization level (which, of course, runs SROA), this [code](https://godbolt.org/z/Eh8qzM6rf) is not optimized.
But I don't see anything that would prevent TRE of this code.
Am I missing something?

This PR would change the code to:
```llvm
declare void @callback(ptr readonly captures(none))

define i32 @recursive_with_callback(i32 %n, i32 %acc) {
entry:
  %data = alloca i32, align 4
  %holder = alloca ptr, align 8
  br label %tailrecurse

tailrecurse:                                      ; preds = %recurse, %entry
  %n.tr = phi i32 [ %n, %entry ], [ %n_dec, %recurse ]
  %acc.tr = phi i32 [ %acc, %entry ], [ %acc_inc, %recurse ]
  store i32 %acc.tr, ptr %data, align 4
  store ptr %data, ptr %holder, align 8
  call void @callback(ptr %holder)
  %cond = icmp eq i32 %n.tr, 0
  br i1 %cond, label %exit, label %recurse

recurse:                                          ; preds = %tailrecurse
  %n_dec = sub i32 %n.tr, 1
  %val = load i32, ptr %data, align 4
  %acc_inc = add i32 %val, 1
  br label %tailrecurse

exit:                                             ; preds = %tailrecurse
  %result = load i32, ptr %data, align 4
  ret i32 %result
}
```

In addition, if I am not mistaken I would like to discuss an example involving `volatile` to understand it better.

comments.author: antoniofrighetto
comments.body:
> I don't think we could perform TRE in this case, however the patch does?

Thinking more on the code I suggested before, despite the function escaping a local pointer, I think it should still be possible to apply TRE and reuse the current stack frame; I can't imagine any meaningful use of the addresses of the stack objects that one would get per each stack frame allocated during recursion.

> If I understand correctly, even with the -O3 optimization level (which, of course, runs SROA), this [code](https://godbolt.org/z/Eh8qzM6rf) is not optimized.

I think it may help start from a real-world snippet (e.g., a Rust one), see if some of the allocas are still there by the time TRE kicks in (and thus proving the usefulness of the patch, probably something along these lines? https://llvm.godbolt.org/z/1cc79xbe4).

> This PR would change the code to:

In principle, this looks a valid refinement to me.

comments.author: nikic
comments.body:
> Thinking more on the code I suggested before, despite the function escaping a local pointer, I think it should still be possible to apply TRE and reuse the current stack frame; I can't imagine any meaningful use of the addresses of the stack objects that one would get per each stack frame allocated during recursion.

I don't think this is true. The `readonly nocapture` only makes a statement about `%holder`, it makes no statement about how that `%data` stored in it can be used. The callback might be looking at its address, or storing the pointer on one iteration and using it on the next, etc.

Okay, I wasn't aware of that semantic aspect. I would say that the PR can be closed without any problems.
